questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If the coefficients of x<sup>7</sup> in $${\left( {{x^2} + {1 \over {bx}}} \right)^{11}}$$ and x<sup>$$-$$7</sup> in $${\left( {{x} - {1 \over {bx^2}}} \right)^{11}}$$, b $$\ne$$ 0, are equal, then the value of b is equal to :
questions.solution:
Coefficient of x7 in $${\left( {{x^2} + {1 \over {bx}}} \right)^{11}}$$ :

General Term = $${}^{11}{C_r}{({x^2})^{11 - r}}.{\left( {{1 \over {bx}}} \right)^r}$$

= $${}^{11}{C_r}{x^{22 - 3r}}.{1 \over {{b^r}}}$$

$$22 - 3r = 7$$

$$r = 5$$

$$\therefore$$ Required Term = $${}^{11}{C_5}.{1 \over {{b^5}}}.{x^7}$$

Coefficient of x$$-$$7 in $${\left( {x - {1 \over {b{x^2}}}} \right)^{11}}$$ :

General Term = $${}^{11}{C_r}{(x)^{11 - r}}.{\left( { - {1 \over {b{x^2}}}} \right)^r}$$

= $${}^{11}{C_r}{x^{11 - 3r}}.{{{{( - 1)}^r}} \over {{b^r}}}$$

$$11 - 3r = - 7$$ $$\therefore$$ $$r = 6$$

$$ \therefore $$ Required Term = $${}^{11}{C_6}.{1 \over {{b^6}}}{x^{ - 7}}$$

According to the question,

$${}^{11}{C_5}.{1 \over {{b^5}}} = {}^{11}{C_6}.{1 \over {{b^6}}}$$

Since, b $$\ne$$ 0 $$\therefore$$ b = 1

where $$\alpha$$ $$\in$$ R, then the value of 16$$\alpha$$ is equal to

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: $50^{\text {th }}$ root of a number $x$ is 12 and $50^{\text {th }}$ root of another number $y$ is 18 . Then the remainder obtained on dividing $(x+y)$ by 25 is ____________.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The remainder left out when $${8^{2n}} - {\left( {62} \right)^{2n + 1}}$$ is divided by 9 is :
questions.solution:
$${8^{2n}} - {\left( {62} \right)^{2n + 1}}$$

= $${\left( {{8^2}} \right)^n} - {\left( {62} \right)^{2n + 1}}$$

= $${\left( {1 + 63} \right)^n} - {\left( {1 - 63} \right)^{2n + 1}}$$

= $$\left( {1 + n.63 + {}^n{C_2}{{.63}^2} + ......} \right)$$
         + $$\left( {1 + {}^{2n + 1}{C_1}.\left( { - 63} \right) + {}^{2n + 1}{C_2}.{{\left( { - 63} \right)}^2} + ......} \right)$$

= 2 + 63$$\left[ {\left( {n + {}^n{C_2} + ....} \right) + \left( { - {}^{2n + 1}{C_1} + {}^{2n + 1}{C_2}.63 + ......} \right)} \right]$$

= 63$$ \times $$[Some integral value] + 2

63$$ \times $$[Some integral value] + 2 by dividing with 9 we will get 2 as remainder as 63 is multiple of 9.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The least value of n for which the number of integral terms in the Binomial expansion of $(\sqrt[3]{7}+\sqrt[12]{11})^n$ is 183, is :
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { General term }={ }^n C_r\left(7^{1 / 3}\right)^{n-r}\left(11^{1 / 12}\right)^r \\ & ={ }^n C_r(7)^{\frac{n-r}{3}}(11)^{r / 12} \end{aligned}$$

For integral terms, $r$ must be multiple of 12

$$\therefore \mathrm{r}=12 \mathrm{k}, \mathrm{k} \in \mathrm{~W}$$

Total values of $\mathrm{r}=183$

Hence $\max r=12(182)$

$$=2184$$

Min value of $n=2184$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The ratio of the coefficient of the middle term in the expansion of (1 + x)<sup>20</sup> and the sum of the coefficients of two middle terms in expansion of (1 + x)<sup>19</sup> is _____________.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If the coefficients of the middle terms in the binomial expansions of $(1+\alpha x)^{26}$ and $(1-\alpha x)^{28}, \alpha \neq 0$, are equal, then the value of $\alpha$ is:
questions.solution:
For a binomial expansion $(a+b)^n$:

  • if $n$ is even, there is only one middle term,

  • and the middle term is the $\left(\dfrac{n}{2}+1\right)$th term.

We are given:

$$ (1+\alpha x)^{26} \quad \text{and} \quad (1-\alpha x)^{28} $$

and the coefficients of their middle terms are equal.

1. Middle term of $(1+\alpha x)^{26}$

Since $26$ is even, the middle term is the $\left(\dfrac{26}{2}+1\right)=14$th term.

The general term in $(1+\alpha x)^{26}$ is

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{26}{r}(1)^{26-r}(\alpha x)^r $$

So the $14$th term corresponds to $r=13$:

$$ T_{14}=\binom{26}{13}(\alpha x)^{13} $$

Hence, the coefficient of the middle term is

$$ \binom{26}{13}\alpha^{13} $$

2. Middle term of $(1-\alpha x)^{28}$

Since $28$ is even, the middle term is the $\left(\dfrac{28}{2}+1\right)=15$th term.

The general term is

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{28}{r}(1)^{28-r}(-\alpha x)^r $$

So the $15$th term corresponds to $r=14$:

$$ T_{15}=\binom{28}{14}(-\alpha x)^{14} $$

Since $14$ is even,

$$ (-\alpha x)^{14}=\alpha^{14}x^{14} $$

Thus, the coefficient of the middle term is

$$ \binom{28}{14}\alpha^{14} $$

3. Equate the coefficients

Given that these coefficients are equal:

$$ \binom{26}{13}\alpha^{13}=\binom{28}{14}\alpha^{14} $$

Since $\alpha\neq 0$, divide both sides by $\alpha^{13}$:

$$ \binom{26}{13}=\binom{28}{14}\alpha $$

So,

$$ \alpha=\frac{\binom{26}{13}}{\binom{28}{14}} $$

4. Simplify

$$ \alpha=\frac{\dfrac{26!}{13!13!}}{\dfrac{28!}{14!14!}} $$

$$ \alpha=\frac{26!}{13!13!}\cdot \frac{14!14!}{28!} $$

Now use

$$ 14!=14\cdot 13! $$

So,

$$ 14!14!=(14\cdot 13!)(14\cdot 13!)=14^2(13!)^2 $$

Thus,

$$ \alpha=\frac{26!\cdot 14^2(13!)^2}{(13!)^2\cdot 28!} =\frac{26!\cdot 14^2}{28!} $$

Also,

$$ 28!=28\cdot 27\cdot 26! $$

Therefore,

$$ \alpha=\frac{14^2}{28\cdot 27} =\frac{14}{2\cdot 27} =\frac{7}{27} $$

$$ \boxed{\alpha=\frac{7}{27}} $$

So, the correct option is D.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The sum of the coefficients of $x^{499}$ and $x^{500}$ in $(1 + x)^{1000} + x(1 + x)^{999} + x^2(1 + x)^{998} + \ldots + x^{1000}$ is :
questions.solution:
Let

$$ S=(1+x)^{1000}+x(1+x)^{999}+x^2(1+x)^{998}+\cdots+x^{1000} $$

Notice that this is a Geometric Progression (GP) with:

  • First term (a): $(1+x)^{1000}$

  • Common ratio $(r): \frac{x}{1+x}$

  • Number of terms ( $n$ ): 1001

The sum of a GP is given by $S=a \frac{\left(1-r^n\right)}{(1-r)}$. Substituting our values:

$$ S=(1+x)^{1000}\left[\frac{1-\left(\frac{x}{1+x}\right)^{1001}}{1-\frac{x}{1+x}}\right] $$

Simplify the denominator: $1-\frac{x}{1+x}=\frac{1+x-x}{1+x}=\frac{1}{1+x}$.

$$ S=(1+x)^{1000} \cdot(1+x)\left[1-\frac{x^{1001}}{(1+x)^{1001}}\right] $$

$$ S=(1+x)^{1001}-x^{1001} $$

Now we need the coefficients of $x^{499}$ and $x^{500}$ in the simplified expression $(1+x)^{1001}- x^{1001}$.

Since $x^{1001}$ only affects the term with power 1001, we only look at $(1+x)^{1001}$.

In the binomial expansion $(1+x)^n$

Coefficient of $x^r$ is ${ }^n C_r$

Coefficient of $x^{499}$ in $S$ :

$$ { }^{1001} C_{499} $$

Coefficient of $x^{500}$ in $S$ :

${ }^{1001} C_{500}$ (The term $x^{1001}$ does not contribute.)

$$ \text { Sum of coefficients } $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} { }^{1001} C_{499}+{ }^{1001} C_{500}={ }^{1002} C_{500} \end{array} $$

[$\text { Using pascal's identity } $

${ }^n C_r+{ }^n C_{r+1}={ }^{n+1} C_{r+1}$]

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $$\alpha=\sum_\limits{k=0}^n\left(\frac{\left({ }^n C_k\right)^2}{k+1}\right)$$ and $$\beta=\sum_\limits{k=0}^{n-1}\left(\frac{{ }^n C_k{ }^n C_{k+1}}{k+2}\right)$$ If $$5 \alpha=6 \beta$$, then $$n$$ equals _______.
questions.solution: $$\begin{aligned} \alpha= & \sum_{k=0}^n \frac{{ }^n C_k \cdot{ }^n C_k}{k+1} \cdot \frac{n+1}{n+1} \\ & =\frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{k=0}^n{ }^{n+1} C_{k+1} \cdot{ }^n C_{n-k} \\ \alpha & =\frac{1}{n+1} \cdot{ }^{2 n+1} C_{n+1} \\ \beta & =\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} C_k \cdot \frac{{ }^n C_{k+1}}{k+2} \frac{n+1}{n+1} \\ & \frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}{ }^n C_{n-k} \cdot{ }^{n+1} C_{k+2} \\ & =\frac{1}{n+1} \cdot{ }^{2 n+1} C_{n+2} \\ \frac{\beta}{\alpha} & =\frac{2 n+1}{2 n+1} C_{n+2} \\ \frac{\beta}{\alpha} & =\frac{2 n+1-(n+2)+1}{n+2}=\frac{5}{6} \\ n & =10 \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The coefficient of $$x^{2012}$$ in the expansion of $$(1-x)^{2008}\left(1+x+x^2\right)^{2007}$$ is equal to _________.
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & (1-x)(1-x)^{2007}\left(1+x+x^2\right)^{2007} \\ & (1-x)\left(1-x^3\right)^{2007} \\ & (1-x)\left({ }^{2007} C_0-{ }^{2007} C_1\left(x^3\right)+\ldots \ldots .\right) \end{aligned}$$

General term

$$\begin{aligned} & (1-x)\left((-1)^r{ }^{2007} C_r x^{3 r}\right) \\ & (-1)^{r 2007} C_r x^{3 r}-(-1)^{r 2007} C_r x^{3 r+1} \\ & 3 r=2012 \\ & r \neq \frac{2012}{3} \\ & 3 r+1=2012 \\ & 3 r=2011 \\ & r \neq \frac{2011}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Hence there is no term containing $$\mathrm{x}^{2012}$$.

So coefficient of $$\mathrm{x}^{2012}=0$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The sum of the coefficients of three consecutive terms in the binomial expansion of $$(1+\mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{n}+2}$$, which are in the ratio $$1: 3: 5$$, is equal to :
questions.solution:
The problem asks for the sum of the coefficients of three consecutive terms in the binomial expansion of $(1+x)^{n+2}$, which are in the ratio 1 : 3 : 5.

Given that the ratios of the coefficients are 1:3:5, we let the terms be $T_r$, $T_{r+1}$, and $T_{r+2}$. The coefficients of these terms are ${ }^{n+2} C_{r-1}$, ${ }^{n+2} C_{r}$, and ${ }^{n+2} C_{r+1}$, respectively.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{T_{r+1}}{T_r}=\frac{{ }^{n+2} C_r}{{ }^{n+2} C_{r-1}}=\frac{n+2-r+1}{r}=\frac{n+3-r}{r}=3 \\\\ & n-4 r+3=0 ......(1) \\\\ & \frac{T_{r+2}}{T_{r+1}}=\frac{{ }^{n+2} C_{r+1}}{{ }^{n+2} C_r}=\frac{(n+2)-(r+1)+1}{r+1}=\frac{n-r+2}{r+1}=\frac{5}{3} \\\\ & 3 n-8 r+1=0 ......(2) \end{aligned} $$

By solving (1) and (2), we get

$\Rightarrow n=5, r=2$

$$ \begin{aligned} T_r+T_{r+1}+T_{r+2} & ={ }^7 C_1+{ }^7 C_2+{ }^7 C_3 \\\\ & =7+21+35=63 \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Suppose $A$ and $B$ are the coefficients of $30^{\text {th }}$ and $12^{\text {th }}$ terms respectively in the binomial expansion of $(1+x)^{2 \mathrm{n}-1}$. If $2 \mathrm{~A}=5 \mathrm{~B}$, then n is equal to:
